question: Why do you need a correlation ID (request ID) in logs?

options.text: To link all logs of one HTTP request -- with parallel requests from different users, logs get interleaved, making it impossible to trace the chain without an ID
For request authentication -- request ID confirms authenticity
options.explanation: Request ID isn't related to authentication. It's a unique identifier for tracking: all logs of one request (middleware, service, DB) contain the same requestId

options.text: To count requests per second
options.explanation: RPS is measured with metrics (Prometheus), not logs. Correlation ID links logs of one request. A single request can generate 10-50 log lines

options.text: It's a GDPR requirement for tracking user data
options.explanation: GDPR doesn't require request IDs. Correlation ID is an engineering practice for debugging. Without it, finding all logs of a specific failed request is impossible

question: What's the difference between liveness and readiness health checks?

options.text: Liveness -- 'is the process alive?' (restart on deadlock), Readiness -- 'can it serve traffic?' (remove from LB when DB is down)
Liveness checks CPU, readiness checks RAM
options.explanation: Neither is directly related to system resources. Liveness answers 'is the process stuck', readiness -- 'are dependencies working (DB, Redis, etc.)'

options.text: Liveness for Kubernetes, readiness for Docker
options.explanation: Both are used in Kubernetes (and Docker health checks). The difference is in action: failed liveness → restart pod, failed readiness → remove from Service (no traffic)

options.text: Liveness runs once per minute, readiness continuously
options.explanation: Interval is configurable (periodSeconds). Typically both check every 10-30 seconds. The difference isn't frequency but consequences of failure

question: Why is Pino faster than Winston?

options.text: Pino uses a minimal JSON serializer and async writing -- up to 5x faster than Winston due to no synchronous transforms
Pino is written in C++, Winston in JavaScript
options.explanation: Both are written in JavaScript. Pino is faster due to architecture: minimal overhead, no transform chains, uses fast JSON.stringify

options.text: Winston logs more data, so it's slower
options.explanation: Data volume depends on configuration, not the library. Pino is faster due to internal architecture: direct JSON write vs transform chain in Winston

options.text: Pino doesn't support multiple transports, so it's faster
options.explanation: Pino supports transports via pino-transport (streams). But the main approach is one stdout stream with external routing (pino-pretty, pino-file)

question: Why is redaction needed in a logger?

options.text: To automatically hide sensitive data (passwords, tokens, card numbers) -- they're replaced with [Redacted] and don't end up in log files
For log compression -- redaction reduces file size
options.explanation: Redaction doesn't compress data. It replaces values with '[Redacted]', which might even be longer than the original. The goal is security, not size

options.text: For JSON format compliance -- some values can't be serialized
options.explanation: JSON serialization works for any strings. Redaction is about security: if log files leak, passwords and tokens won't be compromised

options.text: Redaction speeds up logging by skipping long values
options.explanation: Redaction adds overhead (needs to check paths). It doesn't speed up logging. The goal is preventing sensitive data leaks through logs

question: What should the readiness endpoint return when the DB is unavailable?

options.text: 503 with details: which dependency failed, check responseTime, current uptime
200 -- service is running, DB is just temporarily unavailable
options.explanation: 200 from readiness tells LB: 'send traffic'. But without DB, requests will fail with 500. Readiness should return 503 so LB stops sending traffic

options.text: 500 -- Internal Server Error
options.explanation: 500 means server error. Readiness probe expects 200 (healthy) or non-200 (unhealthy). 503 Service Unavailable is the semantically correct status for 'not ready'

options.text: 404 -- endpoint not found, disable the check
options.explanation: 404 is the wrong status. Readiness endpoint should always respond: 200 = healthy, 503 = not ready. Kubernetes interprets non-200 as unhealthy
